speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Eli Epstein. It's May 12, 1995. Tell me, if you would, where were you September, when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: On September 1, 1939, I was in a Hashomer Hatzair camp in the Tatra Mountains, very close to the Polish border. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think, since the age of seven, I used to go to a Hashomer Hatzairian camp every summer. That took about four weeks and then the rest of the summer I spent with my family and visiting relatives, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember of that day? Do you remember that there was-- do you remember particular memories of, of the war and what you-- your impressions were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just that in the morning, when, when we raised the flag, the usual thing, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH], who was the-- who was the [NON-ENGLISH] and the head of the camp, passed around that the war broke out and that we'll have to close down the camp. And everybody go home because it was not clear what would happen. And it was not clear whether the Germans would be entering Slovakia. So the camp got closed that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Went home by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you arrived home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing, just my father's anxieties were up. And my mother was very much perturbed. It wasn't clear what would happen. Starting in '39, it was quite clear that the Slovaks who were collaborating with the Germans wanted to, to make a separate deal with the Germans to create an independent Slovak state. This was after Munich. This is after the Germans already occupied the Sudetenland and-- where Hitler indicated that he would destroy Czechoslovakia. But in the conversation, it became clear that perhaps Slovakia would be treated differently than, than Bohemia. After all, Slovak did not have the armaments industry. So again, this was part of the rationalization. Well, maybe things are going to be different here in Slovakia than elsewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for a period of time, life continued as before.

speaker: interviewee
text: For a period of time, life continued. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A number of Jews left, not too many. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was really not too many places to go. It was hard to go to Israel. Or that, that was for young people. I mean, the aliyahs accelerated, of course, in-- after '39. But most of the Jews who were elderly and had family and houses just couldn't visualize themselves going somewhere-- picking themselves up and going somewhere else. The Jews who left were wealthy lawyers. Specifically, I remember two families, wealthy lawyers who had connections with, with, with people abroad. And a particular family, the Pecek family, who was a very rich family, bankers, whose children had been educated in England, they just took off and left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those people who had established links abroad and had a base and had money abroad find it very easy to just get a visa and disappear. And so, so that what happened early in 1939. Your father's business continued as before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my father's business continued as before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a safeguard, my father tried to arrange it so that some of his friends, if his business would be expropriated, it would be expropriated by, by people who had a personal relationship with him, which subsequently actually happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he started cultivating-- which is again, the, the approach of the rationalization of the inevitable, started cultivating the right wing so-called Hlinka people associated with the Catholic Church, who would presumably go into favor in the event the Slovak State would come to pass. When, when did you find that there were what might be called incremental, new legislations, new rules that suddenly started to restrict you, your behaviors, your education, your way of life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the, the decisive step was the proclamation of the Slovak State, independent state, in March of 1939. And part of the pledge was that the Slovak State would adopt the Nuremberg laws. In many respects, it was seen as, as a viable solution for many of the Jews. Because they said, well, we are just going to hunker down. And so we'll live with the Nuremberg laws. At least the Nazis will not come because the people we know, these are the people you've always done business with. You can do business with them. And so we're going to try to negotiate incrementally what kind of a deal we can make. And so the first restrictions came in 1939. I have a very interesting collection of documents-- which I donated to the-- to the Holocaust Museum, which, in my opinion, is a very, very unique set of documents-- which I collected after the war in Slovakia, which shows the incrementality of the laws and the degradation and the taking away of Jewish privileges and rights. And, and it shows how the Slovak State, in some respect-- the way the Germans did it except not as brutally-- used a legal process by passing a law saying, well, there can be no Jewish innkeepers. You know, by-- in six months, everybody who is a Jewish innkeeper has to give up their license and hand it over to what's called an 'arizator,' somebody who makes it Aryan, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now you take the economic base of the innkeepers. Of course, half of my uncles were innkeepers. So, so that happens.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then, of course, the war scales up. England enters the war. And everybody else enters the war. The BBC makes broadcast.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you pass another law. Jews cannot have radios Oh, no, Jews cannot have radios that have shortwave. OK, if you have a radio with a shortwave, you have to hand it over to the local police. And you get a receipt. And so I have, in this collection of documents, all of these receipts this particular Jew received.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then that happens. Then you pass another law. There are certain streets where Jews cannot live. So if it's a prominent merchant street, you cannot live on that street. And the law that has been passed by the Slovak parliament shows the towns and the names of the street in the law. It says, on Via [NON-ENGLISH], no Jew can live. Of course, they picked all the prominent Jewish places. So-- OK, so in 1940, we have to move out of the house my father built. It was very traumatic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Required by law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Required by law.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do? What do you remember about that period specific to moving out of the house? Who did you sell the house to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, you didn't sell the house. You couldn't sell the house. You had to give it to-- into a custody of an Aryan. It was-- in other words, it was taken over. There was no deed of sale.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you packed up all your belongings.

speaker: interviewee
text: You packed up. Well, you know, we had a big house. So we had to leave the furniture there because our furniture was built in. And so we had to take, you know, whatever we could. And we had to move from our large, four-bedroom apartment to a one-and-a-half bedroom apartment in an adjoining street, which was not on the list.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sense, then, was because you were getting receipts, that this was temporary, and somehow it would be reversed later on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the whole thing was done-- then-- nobody knew what the war will look like. So we said, well, fine, the war is here. But the war will come to pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somehow we'll survive. We've survived things before. After all, there have been other situations before, where things have come and gone. You know, how bad can it be? And certainly, they're not killing us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since the borders are closed-- because the borders closed after mid-1939. You could only go illegally. But now you couldn't go illegally with grandparents and with children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're stuck. So you've got no choice. So here's a law. You get a receipt. And off you go. Somewhere you refer to this as a longstanding tradition where Jews had to learn to submit.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this part of that whole process?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's part of that thing is that if you have no choice-- and particularly with the closing of the borders, there was no choice. And the fall of France-- by the way, Kubicek, by that time, went to France, and from France, went to Portugal. So he's out there trying to find a legal way of getting us out. Of course, it doesn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're still corresponding by--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we're still corresponding with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: --by writing?

speaker: interviewee
text: By writing. And, you know, there are drops where you send it to somebody and then leave it. There was a, a aborted effort to go to, to Italy, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which was-- to Monte Carlo, which was sort of independent, and stay there and find a ship to go somewhere. You know, by that time, you are ready to go any place that you can. I mean, I don't care it's-- whether Madagascar or Cuba, just let's get out. But you can't. You know, the thing is, sort of, the trap keeps closing and closing. And in retrospect, you could have gone out if you would have taken certain risks. By the time you're ready to take those risks, those risks have already been cut off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you recall any memorable dialogues with either your mother, your father during this period, where they would try to convey some, some articulation of what they were feeling? Did you sense the fear and the panic? Or, or was it just moving into a heightened degree of anxiety?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just heightened anxiety. I mean, there was not very much to explain. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In some respects, many of the contingencies that were-- that my parents were planning and my father was planning, in fact, were not disclosed to us, the children, in the fear that I would sort of disclose things or inadvertently blab it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so actually, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there is very little in there that I could say except an increased apprehension and, and tension in the family, which is an impact on family life.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The, the only thing that I remember was, you know, we have this Persian rug. It's in the main house. We don't want to leave it there. But we now want to give the Persian rug as a graft to somebody who can give us certain privileges. So those kind of discussions. Then we have a Contax camera, which was a very fancy, 35-millimeter camera. Well, you, you start shedding voluntarily certain assets because they are going to be taken away anyway. So now you might as well give them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: The question is, how can you do it legitimately? Because throughout 1939 and 1949, you have this flood of paper coming in, where you have to fill out your property and your relationship, and increasingly are forced to, to, to disclose what you have and what you think and who do you talk to, and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing that really overwhelms me in my memory of the '40, '41, particularly '42 is the increased bureaucratization. In other words, the, the, the genocide and the preparation for the genocide is really driven by a bureaucracy. We just execute laws. In fact, in subsequent legal actions that have to deal with some of the recovery of our property, you know, from a legal standpoint, the thing is legitimate because even America recognized the acts of law passed by the Slovak government. I mean, it's a legitimate government. It has a-- it has a legislature. The courts approve it. The courts review the decisions. And you fill out the forms. And you have no recourse. So it is this understanding how the machinery of the state can be used to sort of strip people out of existence which becomes a very significant influence on my outlook. Much of the Holocaust [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] discussion deals with out-and-out brutal beating, shooting, murder, and what have you. There has been very little given in terms of documentation of what proceeded that process, where the machinery of the police state, in fact, starts preparing people for the Final Solution. And it's done in a very deliberate, studied, and organized way,

speaker: interviewer
text: Always the key being incrementally so that it's almost imperceptible at some point.

speaker: interviewee
text: In addition to that, in addition to that, what is interesting, the state always sees to it that there are people who have become stakeholders of this thing. So when you decide that you cannot live on [NON-ENGLISH], the immediate issue is, well, who is going to move into these Jewish houses?

speaker: interviewer
text: And so much of that discussion from that period really deals with property. How do you divide the property? Who will get the claims of property? You must understand much of the history. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this goes back to the French Revolution, as well, is that when you change the regime, who is-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who will be the recipient of the spoils? So this is not just a unique Jewish experience. You find it in many other revolutionary situations. Despite all of these ideologies, there is a co-optation of a new class of people, a new, rich, ruling class who comes in who benefits from it, and who then become an executor and a stakeholder of the process. Let me move back a little bit to-- I'm, I'm, I'm going to guess 1940-- when you stopped being able to go to school. Was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what had happened? That's clearly a pivotal moment in your life as a child. You're no longer allowed to attend school. What did you sense? And what did you do at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father saw to it that I had private tutors. In fact, I must say that my education in 1940 and '41 particularly greatly improved. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were friends of my father-- one, who was a teacher of mathematics, who was actually a professor of mathematics. Of course, these student-- these professors did not get much money. So they always were tutoring

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father saw to it that I learned English, mathematics. So I continued very, very good education. And, in fact, I acquired certain studying habits, which were, in fact, superior and served me very well in the future.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because most of the time children spend in classes are wasted and utterly boring because you sit in a group of 20, 30 people listening to stuff which is immaterial and most likely something that you really are not interested in doing anyway. Whereas, I remember 1940 and '41 as an opportunity to acquire the habit of independent learning.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- at what period did you have to start wearing the Star of David, the yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started wearing the yellow star early in 1942. In other words, this was-- as a precursor for the deportations to make sure that you nab everybody, you first enforce the yellow star rule.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember that being an issue at home when it first came about?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my, my-- you know, the question was, what coat? I mean, there was a limited supply of yellow stars. So the question is which jacket do you put it on?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, then, there was these little minutiae. Do you-- do you put it so if you have a white collar, you know? Can you cover part of the yellow star by saying well, it couldn't fit, and so forth? So, so I remember those kind of details. Was that a source of embarrassment or pain when, when you would have to wear that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, oh yes, it, it was a, a sense of embarrassment. But by that time, of course, you knew you were stripped. I mean, you have gone through two years of stripping-- you know, no radio, no books, no movies, no music, prescribed places where you can live, curfews. No fur coats was a big deal, no fur coats-- a law that Jews cannot have a fur coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, if you could, when the first deportations began, and how that started to occur.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first deportations began in 1942. The deportation lists were made up by the Judenrat, in other words, the local Kommandatura, which was basically a German skeleton, cruel, only two or three Gestapo officers who were there anyway handed over, well, we want so many Jews, you know, so many cars. You know, we have-- it was done through production scheduling. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By the way, one of the most interesting contributions I made to, to the Holocaust Museum was to identify the punch card, the counting machines, IBM machines, that were used to schedule transports. I, in fact, helped the Holocaust Museum to locate an authentic German IBM machine made in 1932, which was identical to the machine that was used for scheduling transports.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans, in fact, hit a transportation problem. You know, you know, how many-- because of the military movement, how many trains can I allocate to this thing? So basically the German handed over to the local Garda, which was the black shirts-- the, the Hlinka guard and the local police. So it was a combination. There was always a policeman who was part of the established order. And there was a legal process.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Garda handed it over, made sure that the policeman does, does not do anything improper. And of course, the Garda was there particularly to make sure that the property of the Jews who went to-- in a transport immediately gets sequestered and properly acquired because it was a process by which-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a process by which, in fact, the state acquired wealth for distribution to their faithful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- excuse me, [LAUGHS] that's a woodpecker. No problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. It's-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're, we're-- I'm trying to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry for the disturbance.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's no problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we have a bird out there who sounds like a machine gun. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: No problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm trying to recapture the, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the first deportations.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Germans have a headquarters. We know where it is. They hand over a requisition list. Here are so many cars. Fill them with Jews. Here is the quota. Goes to the Garda. The Garda hands it over to the police. The police comes to, to the-- to the Jewish community leaders and says, OK, we need 320.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they selected?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we need-- and, and they give qualifications. They have to be this-and-this age. And, of course, immediately what you start with in '42, you first take the young men who could possibly offer resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So under the guise of deportation for labor purposes, we want [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] young men who are capable of doing work. And their age has to be 19 through 24. We want 320 of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's handed over to the Jewish community. And by that time, you have these very elaborate demographic surveys, you know, by age, city, location, what have you. And the Jewish community then prepares a list. Here are 320 that we can offer to you. And so everybody in the community, the moment a requisition comes out, knows that this is going to happen, and the transport is leaving in two days. And so that's when the bargaining begins. You know, my son is a son of a doctor. And he's not capable of, of doing this and that. So-- and sometimes injuries are actually perpetrated for that purpose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the case of my sister, it worked. My sister's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] right hand was put into a cast early in 1942. And to, to this day, her hand is really disabled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of the cast?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because she was immobilized for an extended period of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet, of course, people were deported. What, what was the impact on the community that stayed behind? In other words, those families who lost their, their, their family members.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they hoped that the-- you know, please write. Where are you going? We're going to a labor camp. We are going to work on German military this-- and, of course, the pictures come in. The propaganda comes in with pictures of Jews sewing, you know, uniforms, and what have you. So there was very strong collateral propaganda going on. A request for food parcels, you know-- and the thing initially is done very officially. And there's confirmation. Yes, we received the food parcels. And we thank you very much. Send us some more, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so in addition to that, the Slovak Republic says, well, there are some labor camps we want on our own-- partially with the Jewish community cooperation, the deportations to the Slovak camp, which stay in Slovakia and where you can see them. Because those camps in Poland you couldn't see.

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the, there are three camps in Slovakia, at least, that are visible to you. And surely, people live in barracks. But at least they go where you can observe them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you can see them. And that is taken as a credit against the deportation. So much of the discussion when these requisitions come is, well, can I get credit for this man to go to Novaky instead of going to, to, to, to Poland? And now, at this period in your own family, were there some relationships which your father harvested or cultivated, made use of, that enabled you or other family members to avoid deportations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happens when the deportations happen, this is when, when the thing becomes just very, very, very obvious to my father. My father has a fairly good set of connections, which suggest that the deportations to Poland, in fact, may be death camps, although nobody knows that for sure. Nobody really wants to believe it anyway because letters still keep coming back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Turns out that in 1942, the President of the Slovak Republic, Monsignor Tiso, who is a collaborator, institutes a special kind of-- what's called a presidential exemption program, which protects certain Jews from deportation. It's what's called a white card.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, you have a Jewish card. And then you can have what's called a 'white card.' So now, the objective of every Jew who's left in Slovakia is, how do I get a white card, you know? We're, we're going to end the second tape--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and resume discussions on the third tape--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --pick up on the white card.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
